headline: Idaho Territory Created, Encompassing All of Present-Day Montana, 1863
body:
Well now, gather 'round, folks, and let ol' Mark tell you a tale of how a piece of this grand, untamed West got itself a name, if only for a spell. It was the fourth day of March, back in '63, when the good ol' United States Congress, bless their busy hearts, decided to draw some new lines on the map. They carved out a colossal chunk of land, bigger than a whale's belly, and declared it the Idaho Territory. This wasn't just some small-time parcel, mind you. This new territory swallowed up all of what we now call Idaho, every last acre of Montana, and a goodly portion of Wyoming besides. It was a territory so vast, a man could ride for weeks and still be within its borders, though I reckon most folks were too busy digging for gold or dodging arrows to pay much mind to where one imaginary line ended and another began.

Now, this wasn't done on a whim, no sir. The Washington and Dakota Territories, which had been holding these lands, were getting a bit unwieldy, like a steer too big for its pen. The Civil War was raging back East, a terrible, bloody business, but out here, the real war was against the wilderness, and sometimes, against each other. Gold had been discovered, you see, and where there's gold, there's a rush, and where there's a rush, there's a need for some semblance of law and order, or at least a government to collect taxes. So, President Lincoln, a man with a heap of troubles on his plate, put his signature to the act, and just like that, Idaho was born, a sprawling infant with a future as uncertain as a poker game.

But here's the rub, and it's a fine piece of irony, if you ask me. Even as they were drawing these grand, sweeping lines, the wise men in Congress knew this arrangement wouldn't last. They put a little clause in the Organic Act, a bit of foresight, you might say, that read: "That nothing in this act contained shall be construed to inhibit the government of the United States from dividing said territory or changing its boundaries in such manner and at such time as Congress shall deem convenient and proper, or from attaching any portion of said territory to any other state or territory of the United States." A clear sign, that was, that this Idaho Territory was just a temporary lodging, a way station on the road to something else. And sure enough, barely a year later, Montana, with its own gold strikes and its own brand of rough-and-tumble pioneers, would break away, a separate entity demanding its own destiny. It just goes to show you, when it comes to drawing lines on a map out West, things were always in flux, like a river changing its course, or a politician changing his mind.
